subsystem: libgadu (Gadu-Gadu instant-messaging protocol client library). This is gg_change_status_descr(), the client-side routine that changes the local user's presence/status and its free-text description and marshals that change into a GG_NEW_STATUS80 protocol packet sent to the GG server. It processes the caller-supplied status code, the session's status flags, and a status-description string, recoding it to UTF-8 and framing it into the on-wire struct gg_new_status80.
provenance: Identified by the pervasive gg_/GG_ naming from libgadu: the function gg_change_status_descr, struct gg_new_status80, the packet id GG_NEW_STATUS80, session type struct gg_session, helpers gg_debug_session, gg_encoding_convert, gg_send_packet, the byte-order helper gg_fix32, the constants GG_ENCODING_CP1250/GG_ENCODING_UTF8, GG_STATE_CONNECTED/GG_STATE_DISCONNECTING, GG_PROTOCOL_110, GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE, and the macro GG_S_NA. The Polish in-code comment (line 37) and the CP1250<->UTF8 conversion confirm the Gadu-Gadu (Polish IM) origin. The sample tag 'primevul_c_train_3405_fixed' marks this as the patched revision.
trust_boundary: This is primarily an OUTBOUND path: it builds a packet to send, so the classic 'parse hostile wire bytes' surface is inverted here. The direct inputs are the local caller's `status` (int) and `descr` (const char *) plus session state in `sess`. `descr` may originate from a UI, a script, or a relayed message and is therefore semi-trusted; its length is the security-relevant quantity because it is copied verbatim into the wire frame and its byte count is advertised in p.description_size. The dual, latent boundary is the PEER/receiver: struct gg_new_status80 with its description_size field is the wire contract that a receiving libgadu (or the server) parses, so the clamp enforced here is what protects those parsers from an oversized length. `sess` is a trusted local handle but is used before it is null-checked.

what_the_code_does.code: int gg_change_status_descr(struct gg_session *sess, int status, const char *descr)
what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point. `sess` is the opaque per-connection session handle that carries connection state, encoding, protocol version, and status flags. `status` is an integer presence code (available/away/invisible/etc., possibly OR'd with description/voice flags). `descr` is the user's free-text status message (may be NULL). The function returns 0/positive on success or -1 on error with errno set. All three parameters are attacker-relevant to differing degrees: `descr`'s length drives the packet payload size, and `sess`'s fields gate behaviour.

Local state. `p` is the on-wire GG_NEW_STATUS80 header struct (fields status, flags, description_size seen below at lines 42-44) built on the stack. `gen_descr` holds a heap buffer if the description is recoded to UTF-8; it is initialized NULL so the unconditional free() at line 57 is safe when no conversion happened. `descr_len` (signed int) will hold the byte length of the description that is both advertised in the header and passed as the copy length. `descr_null_len` is 0 or 1 and controls whether a trailing NUL byte is appended for newer protocols. `res` captures the send result.

what_the_code_does.code: gg_debug_session(sess, GG_DEBUG_FUNCTION, "** gg_change_status_descr(%p, %d, \"%s\");\n", sess, status, descr);
what_the_code_does.explanation: A debug/trace log emitted at function entry. It passes `sess` as the first argument (gg_debug_session selects a per-session debug handler/level from it) and formats `descr` with %s. Crucially this executes BEFORE the `if (!sess)` guard at line 12 and before `descr` is proven non-NULL, so both the handle and the string are consumed by the logging path while still potentially NULL.
Null-handle guard: if no session was supplied, set errno to EFAULT (bad address) and fail. This is the correct defensive check, but it is positioned after the line-10 log that already used `sess`.
State gate: a status change may only be sent on a fully connected session. If not GG_STATE_CONNECTED, fail with ENOTCONN. This prevents writing packets into a half-open or unauthenticated session.

Records the requested status into the session so the local library keeps a coherent view of presence even before/independent of the send result. The raw `status` int is stored verbatim; there is no range validation, but it is a local bookkeeping field.

Charset normalization. The GG_NEW_STATUS80 wire format carries UTF-8, so if the session's local encoding is not already UTF-8, the description is recoded from CP1250 to UTF-8. gg_encoding_convert allocates a new heap buffer, whose pointer is captured in both `gen_descr` (for later free) and `descr` (now pointing at the converted copy). The -1,-1 arguments mean 'input length unknown (NUL-terminated), output length unbounded'. The return is null-checked: allocation/conversion failure returns -1. Note this early return happens before any resource other than gen_descr (which is NULL on this path) is held, so it does not leak.

Length computation and the core safety clamp (this is the substance of the '_fixed' patch). When a description exists, its length is measured with strlen and then CLAMPED so it can never exceed GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE — this is the invariant that keeps the advertised description_size and the copied payload within the protocol's maximum, protecting any parser that assumes a bounded description. If descr is NULL, it is replaced with an empty string so downstream code (strlen already skipped, and the send) never dereferences NULL and sends a zero-length description. The Polish XXX comment warns that the clamp may cut in the middle of a multi-byte UTF-8 sequence, producing a truncated/invalid final character.

Marshals the fixed header of struct gg_new_status80. gg_fix32 performs host-to-wire byte-order conversion (GG uses little-endian on the wire) on 32-bit fields. p.status carries the presence code, p.flags the session's status flags, and p.description_size the (now-clamped) length of the description bytes that will immediately follow the header in the packet. The receiver uses description_size to know how many trailing bytes to read, so its correctness is a parsing-safety contract for the peer.

Protocol-version branch. For GG_PROTOCOL_110 and newer, the flags field is overwritten with the fixed value 0x00000014 (a protocol-mandated status-flags constant) and descr_null_len is set to 1, meaning a single trailing NUL byte will be appended after the description so the newer server sees a NUL-terminated description field. Older protocols omit that terminator (descr_null_len stays 0).

what_the_code_does.code:
res = gg_send_packet(sess, GG_NEW_STATUS80, 
			&p, sizeof(p), 
			descr, descr_len,
			"\x00", descr_null_len,
			NULL);
what_the_code_does.explanation: Assembles and transmits the packet via the variadic gg_send_packet, which takes (session, packet_type, then a NULL-terminated list of {pointer, length} chunks). Chunk 1 is the fixed header `&p` of sizeof(p) bytes; chunk 2 is the description body `descr` of descr_len bytes; chunk 3 is a one-byte NUL string of length descr_null_len (0 or 1). The trailing NULL ends the argument list. The security-critical coupling here is that descr_len must be a correct, non-negative count of readable bytes at `descr`, because gg_send_packet will read exactly that many bytes from the buffer.

Releases the UTF-8 conversion buffer if one was allocated at line 25. Because gen_descr was initialized to NULL and free(NULL) is a no-op, this is safe on the path where no conversion occurred. Note `descr` may still alias `gen_descr` here, but it is not used again after the send, so no use-after-free arises.

Post-send bookkeeping. GG_S_NA(status) tests whether the requested status is the 'not available'/offline variant; if so, the session is marked as entering the disconnecting state and a disconnect timeout is armed so the connection tears down cleanly after the offline status is delivered.
Returns the result of gg_send_packet to the caller (bytes/status from the send, or an error code). The function's overall success is therefore the success of transmitting the framed status packet.

what_could_go_wrong.code: gg_debug_session(sess, GG_DEBUG_FUNCTION, "** gg_change_status_descr(%p, %d, \"%s\");\n", sess, status, descr);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Use-before-null-check. This trace call passes `sess` to gg_debug_session and formats `descr` with %s, yet it runs BEFORE the `if (!sess)` guard at line 12 and before `descr` is confirmed non-NULL. If gg_debug_session dereferences `sess` (to read its per-session debug handler/level) a NULL `sess` yields a NULL-pointer dereference and crash at the log site — exactly the 'log macro dereferences a possibly-NULL handle' pattern. Independently, passing a NULL `descr` to a %s conversion is undefined behaviour; glibc prints '(null)' but other libc implementations crash. The invariant the implementation must uphold is: never consume `sess` or `descr` before their NULL guards; either move the log after line 15 or make gg_debug_session/its format arguments NULL-tolerant. Latent — depends on gg_debug_session's body (not shown).

Signed truncation / one-sided clamp. strlen returns size_t but is stored into the signed int `descr_len`. On a 64-bit host a description longer than INT_MAX (2 GiB) truncates on assignment and can land as a negative int. The clamp only bounds the UPPER side (`> GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE`), so a negative value slips through unchanged. That negative descr_len is then advertised as p.description_size = gg_fix32(descr_len) (a bogus huge unsigned on the wire) and passed as the length argument to gg_send_packet at line 53, where it is interpreted as a size and can drive a massive out-of-bounds read of the source buffer. The invariant: measure length in an unsigned/size_t type and reject or clamp with `< 0` handling before use. Real-world exploitability is low (a 2 GiB status description is implausible), but it is a genuine sign-conversion defect (CWE-197/CWE-190).

Wire-contract coupling for struct gg_new_status80.description_size. The advertised description_size and the actual number of description bytes sent are both `descr_len` and must always agree, and both must be bounded by GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE. This is precisely the invariant the '_fixed' patch enforces at lines 34-35: without the clamp, an oversized description_size flows onto the wire and any RECEIVING libgadu/server that copies description_size bytes into a fixed-size status buffer suffers an out-of-bounds write (CWE-787/CWE-120). So the danger is latent in the PEER's parser, and this code's clamp is the guard that keeps peers safe; a regression that removed or weakened the clamp would re-open a remote overflow in receivers. Confirm the clamp constant matches the receiver's buffer capacity.

UTF-8-unaware truncation (acknowledged by the XXX comment: 'remember not to cut in the middle of a utf-8 character'). Because the clamp trims to a byte count without respecting multi-byte sequence boundaries, the last character of an over-length description can be cut mid-sequence, emitting a malformed UTF-8 status to the server and every contact who receives it. This is not memory-unsafe here, but it is an input-validation/robustness defect (CWE-20) that can trip strict UTF-8 validators or renderers downstream. The invariant: truncate on a code-point boundary. Low security impact, primarily a correctness/robustness concern.

summary: This is the patched libgadu gg_change_status_descr(): a status-packet builder whose one real safety mechanism — clamping the description length to GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE before advertising it in gg_new_status80.description_size and copying it — is present and correct, so the outbound path is low risk. Residual issues are latent and defensive: an entry-point debug log that consumes `sess`/`descr` before their NULL guards (possible NULL deref), a signed-int strlen truncation that the one-sided clamp cannot catch, and a byte-wise (UTF-8-unaware) truncation. No high-severity memory-safety bug is present in the shown code.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: int gg_change_status_descr(struct gg_session *sess, int status, const char *descr)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point / trust boundary; descr length and sess NULL-ness are security-relevant.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: struct gg_new_status80 p (fields status, flags, description_size)
reviewed_anchors.reason: On-wire header; description_size must equal the bytes sent and stay within GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE — the peer-parser contract.
Local heap pointer, NULL-initialized, null-checked at line 27 and freed unconditionally (free(NULL) safe) at line 57.
Signed length; strlen truncation to negative int is not caught by the upper-only clamp and flows to description_size and the send length.
Only ever 0 or 1; bounds a one-byte trailing NUL, no arithmetic hazard.
Consumes sess and %s-formats descr before their NULL guards — latent NULL dereference.
Correct NULL guard; only concern is it runs after the line-10 log, captured in that finding.
Proper connection-state gate; prevents sending on half-open sessions.
Local bookkeeping field assignment of a scalar; no buffer or length contract.
Allocation result is null-checked (line 27) and freed (line 57); conversion correctness is internal to that helper (not shown).
This IS the safety invariant/patch; correct on the upper bound but one-sided (no negative guard) and byte-wise (UTF-8-unaware).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: /* XXX ... nie ucinać w środku znaku utf-8 */ (line 37)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Documents mid-codepoint truncation producing malformed UTF-8 on the wire (input-validation/robustness).
Byte-order marshaling of the wire header; description_size correctness is the peer-parser contract (covered by the struct finding).
Version branch selecting fixed flags and enabling a trailing NUL; deterministic, no attacker-controlled length.
Fixed protocol constant; no untrusted data.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: gg_send_packet(sess, GG_NEW_STATUS80, &p, sizeof(p), descr, descr_len, "\x00", descr_null_len, NULL) (lines 51-55)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Variadic {ptr,len} framing; descr/descr_len must be consistent and non-negative — the sink for the descr_len sign issue.
NULL-safe free of the conversion buffer; descr aliases it but is unused afterward, so no use-after-free.
Macro test on a local scalar to arm disconnect; no memory or length contract.
Local state/timeout assignment of constants; no untrusted influence.

findings.title: Session handle and description used by entry-point debug log before their NULL guards
findings.code: gg_debug_session(sess, GG_DEBUG_FUNCTION, "** gg_change_status_descr(%p, %d, \"%s\");\n", sess, status, descr);
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — the body of gg_debug_session; the call site is this file, line 10
findings.invariant: Neither `sess` nor `descr` may be dereferenced/consumed before the `if (!sess)` (line 12) and `if (descr)` (line 31) guards; gg_debug_session and the %s argument must tolerate NULL.
findings.failure_mode: A caller passing sess==NULL reaches gg_debug_session at line 10 before the line-12 guard; if the logger reads sess->debug fields it dereferences NULL and crashes. Separately, descr==NULL passed to %s is undefined behaviour and crashes on libc implementations that do not special-case NULL. Denial of service at the log site.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether gg_debug_session dereferences its `sess` argument (per-session handler/level) and whether the platform libc prints or crashes on %s with NULL; if either is true, move the log below line 15 or NULL-guard the arguments.

findings.title: Signed strlen truncation bypasses the one-sided length clamp
findings.anchor: int descr_len; descr_len = strlen(descr); if (descr_len > GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE) descr_len = GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE;
findings.bug_class: Signed integer truncation / one-sided bound (negative length reaches copy)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0184_cwe310.c (lines 32-35), sink in gg_send_packet (file not provided)
findings.invariant: The description length must be measured and stored in an unsigned/size_t type and validated as within [0, GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE] before it is advertised in p.description_size and used as the gg_send_packet copy length.
findings.failure_mode: If strlen(descr) exceeds INT_MAX, the assignment into signed int descr_len truncates and may become negative; the clamp only tests the upper bound (`>`), so a negative value passes through, is byte-swapped into p.description_size (a huge unsigned on the wire), and is handed to gg_send_packet at line 53 as a length — driving an out-of-bounds read of the source buffer / oversized advertised size. Requires a >2 GiB description, so real-world risk is low, but it is a genuine sign-conversion defect.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether descr can ever exceed INT_MAX bytes on the target platform and how gg_send_packet interprets a negative/huge length argument (size_t promotion).

findings.title: gg_new_status80.description_size / payload length must stay clamped to protect the peer parser
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write in receiver if advertised length exceeds fixed status buffer (latent, regression-guarded)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — the receiving/parsing side that copies description_size bytes; guard lives here at lines 34-35
findings.invariant: The advertised p.description_size must always equal the number of description bytes actually sent AND be <= GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE, and GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE must not exceed the receiver's fixed status-description buffer capacity.
findings.failure_mode: Without the line-34/35 clamp (the substance of this '_fixed' patch), an over-length description would advertise a description_size larger than a receiving client's/server's fixed status buffer; a receiver copying description_size bytes without its own bound overflows that buffer (remote OOB write). Here the clamp upholds the contract; the finding flags the contract so a future regression that removes/weakens the clamp is recognized as re-opening a remote overflow in peers.
findings.what_to_confirm: That GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE equals or is below the receiver's status-description buffer size, and that no code path advertises description_size without also transmitting exactly that many bytes.

findings.title: Byte-wise clamp truncates UTF-8 mid-character (malformed wire string)
findings.anchor: if (descr_len > GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE) descr_len = GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE; /* XXX ... utf-8 */
findings.bug_class: Improper input validation / encoding truncation (invalid UTF-8 emitted)
findings.invariant: Truncation of a UTF-8 status description must land on a code-point boundary so the emitted string is always well-formed UTF-8.
findings.failure_mode: An over-length UTF-8 description clamped to exactly GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE bytes can be cut in the middle of a multi-byte sequence (the code author's own XXX note), sending a malformed final character to the server and all recipients. Not memory-unsafe on this side, but strict UTF-8 validators/renderers downstream may reject or mishandle it. Low impact.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE truncation is later adjusted to a UTF-8 boundary before send, or whether the raw byte cut is what reaches the wire.

audit_checklist: Confirm gg_debug_session (line 10) does not dereference `sess` and that the platform libc tolerates %s with a NULL `descr`; otherwise move the trace below the line-12/line-31 NULL guards.
Verify GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE is <= the fixed status-description buffer size used by every receiving libgadu/server that parses gg_new_status80.description_size.
Change descr_len to size_t (or add a `< 0` guard) so a >INT_MAX strlen cannot become negative and slip past the upper-only clamp into p.description_size and the gg_send_packet length.

Ensure the description_size advertised in `p` always equals the exact byte count passed as descr_len to gg_send_packet on every branch (protocol >=110 adds a NUL but must not change description_size).
Confirm the clamp truncates on a UTF-8 code-point boundary before send so no malformed multi-byte character is emitted.

Check that gg_encoding_convert cannot return a non-NULL but internally inconsistent buffer whose strlen mismatches its allocation, and that free(gen_descr) at line 57 covers every early-return path (it does not run on the line-27/28 early return, which is fine because gen_descr is the just-failed allocation).
